title: Simulation of Non-Hermitian Hamiltonians with Bivariate Quantum Signal Processing
abstract: We achieve query-optimal quantum simulations of non-Hermitian Hamiltonians $H_{\mathrm{eff}} = H_R + iH_I$, where $H_R$ is Hermitian and $H_I \succeq 0$, using a bivariate extension of quantum signal processing (QSP) with non-commuting signal operators. The algorithm encodes the interaction-picture Dyson series as a polynomial on the bitorus, implemented through a structured multivariable QSP (M-QSP) circuit. A constant-ratio condition guarantees scalar angle-finding for M-QSP circuits with arbitrary non-commuting signal operators. A degree-preserving sum-of-squares spectral factorization permits scalar complementary polynomials in two variables. Angles are deterministically calculated in a classical precomputation step, running in $\mathcal{O}(d_R \cdot d_I)$ classical operations. Operator norms $α_R\,,β_I$ contribute additively with query complexity $\mathcal{O}((α_R + β_I)T + \log(1/\varepsilon)/\log\log(1/\varepsilon))$ matching an information-theoretic lower bound in the separate-oracle model, where $H_R$ and $H_I$ are accessed through independent block encodings. The postselection success probability is $e^{-2β_I T}\|e^{-iH_{\mathrm{eff}}T}|ψ_0\rangle\|^2\cdot (1 - \mathcal{O}(\varepsilon))$, decomposing into a state-dependent factor $\|e^{-iH_{\mathrm{eff}}T}|ψ_0\rangle\|^2$ from the intrinsic barrier and an $e^{-2β_I T}$ overhead from polynomial block-encoding.
summary: The paper presents a new quantum algorithm, Bivariate Quantum Signal Processing, for the optimal simulation of non-Hermitian Hamiltonians. It proves that the proposed method matches the fundamental limits of query complexity and provides a practical way to compute the necessary circuit parameters using a combination of recursive algebraic methods and gradient-based optimization.
main_problem: Achieving query-optimal quantum simulations of non-Hermitian Hamiltonians while addressing the fundamental 'postselection barrier' and the complexity of finding rotation angles in multivariable QSP.
main_result: The paper introduces Bivariate Quantum Signal Processing (M-QSP) that achieves the information-theoretic lower bound for query complexity and establishes a tight two-layer bound on the postselection success probability.
method: A bivariate extension of Quantum Signal Processing (M-QSP) using a sum-of-squares (SOS) spectral factorization and a two-stage optimization strategy (recursive angle-finding followed by FFT-based gradient refinement).
model_or_system: A non-Hermitian Hamiltonian H_eff = H_R + iH_I, where H_R is Hermitian and H_I is positive semidefinite, representing non-unitary dynamics such as no-jump trajectories in Lindblad master equations.
key_observables: Postselection success probability, query complexity, and the operator norm of the propagator.
important_parameters: Operator norms alpha_R and beta_I, evolution time T, error tolerance epsilon, and the bidegree (d_R, d_I) of the bivariate polynomial.
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonian components are accessed via a separate-oracle model with independent block-encodings; the algorithm assumes the existence of a bivariate polynomial approximation on the bitorus.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes fundamental lower bounds on query complexity and postselection probability, then introduces the Bivariate M-QSP algorithm, details the mathematical framework for SOS decomposition and angle-finding, and concludes with complexity analyses and optimization strategies.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics, as it provides an optimal algorithmic framework for simulating dissipative dynamics, gain/loss systems, and the no-jump trajectories of the Lindblad equation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. “Residual” on both panels denotes the relative L2 norm ∥PG(Θ) −Ptarget∥2/∥Ptarget∥2 evaluated on an N × N bitorus FFT grid. Left: Dyson-target benchmark, αRT = 0.8, βIT = 0.4. Purple: SOS verification error maxT2 |1−|P|2 −|A|2 −|B|2|. Orange: peel residual from canonical initialization. Grey crosses: 8 multistart restarts per bidegree, and coincident crosses are restarts that converged to the same spurious basin. Green diamonds: best refined residual (the c∞estimate), pinned near 0.1–0.4 across d ∈[4, 10]. The dotted “Target tol” line at 10−10 is the achievable floor for the on-manifold experiment in the right panel. For the Dyson target it sits below c∞and is included here for...

FIG. 3. Query counts for the Eckart-barrier benchmark (αRT = 338, ε = 10−3). Bars give the total oracle-query com- plexity Q for Dyson LCU [55] (segmented, Sec. X), the M-QSP method of this work (Sec. XI), and the information-theoretic lower bound Qlower = αRT + βIT + log(1/ε)/ log log(1/ε). (a) Weak-dissipation regime (βIT ≈16): M-QSP achieves Q = 407, a factor of 1.57× below Dyson LCU (Q = 640) and only 1.14× above the lower bound (Qlower ≈357). (b) Strong- dissipation regime (βIT ≈338): the log(1/ε) segmenta- tion penalty of Dyson LCU dominates, giving Q = 5408 vs Q = 1282 for M-QSP (4.22× reduction) while the lower bound is Qlower ≈680.
